title: Word Processing Fundamentals
obj: Students will be able to create, format, and save word processing documents using a modern word processor, applying text formatting, paragraph styles, and document structure.
content: ribbon/toolbar layout; document view modes; zoom; ruler; status bar
font face, size, bold/italic/underline, color, highlight, clear formatting
alignment (left/center/right/justify), line spacing, indentation, paragraph spacing
bulleted lists, numbered lists, multilevel lists; list indent/outdent
Ctrl+Z, autosave, Save vs. Save As; .docx vs. .pdf vs. .odt; compatibility mode
Ctrl+Home/End, Find & Replace (Ctrl+H), word count, spell check
assess: Formatting exercise: format a provided plain-text document to match a given formatted sample
Short answer: what is the difference between character formatting and paragraph formatting?
Practical: save a document in both .docx and .pdf format and explain when each is appropriate

title: Creating Professional Documents
obj: Students will be able to create polished professional documents using styles, headers/footers, tables, and images in a word processor.
content: applying heading styles (H1/H2/H3); body text style; modifying a style; why styles matter for consistency
inserting header/footer; different first page; page number formatting
inserting tables, merging cells, formatting borders, sorting table data, table styles
inserting images, wrapping text, resizing, alt text for accessibility
margins, orientation, paper size, columns, page breaks vs. section breaks
enabling track changes; accepting/rejecting; adding comments; collaboration workflow
assess: Document creation project: produce a two-page professional report with title, heading styles, a table, and a footer with page numbers
Short answer: why should you use heading styles rather than manually bolding and increasing font size?
Peer review exercise: use track changes to mark up a classmate's document with suggested edits

title: Spreadsheet Fundamentals
obj: Students will be able to enter and organize data in a spreadsheet, apply basic formatting, and navigate common spreadsheet interface features.
content: rows, columns, cells, cell references (A1 notation); Name Box; Formula Bar; sheet tabs
typing text vs. numbers vs. dates; AutoFill for series; data validation basics
number formats (currency, percentage, date), text alignment, cell borders and fill color, wrap text
entering formulas (=A1+B1); order of operations; relative vs. absolute references ($A$1)
single-column sort; multi-level sort; AutoFilter; filtering by value
.xlsx vs. .csv vs. .ods; when to use each; protecting a workbook with a password
assess: Data entry lab: enter a provided dataset (20 rows), apply currency and date formatting, and sort by date
Formula exercise: write formulas to calculate total, average, and percentage in a budget spreadsheet
Short answer: what is the difference between a relative and an absolute cell reference?

title: Spreadsheet Functions for Financial Documents
obj: Students will be able to use built-in spreadsheet functions to perform calculations for budgets, invoices, and financial summaries.
content: =SUM(range), =AVERAGE(range); using ranges vs. individual cells; AutoSum shortcut
=IF(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false); nested IF; common business uses
looking up values in a table; exact vs. approximate match; XLOOKUP as modern replacement
=PMT for loan payments; =SUM for totals; =ROUND for currency rounding
invoice template, budget tracker, grade calculator; applying functions to real forms
assess: Invoice lab: build a 10-line invoice template using SUM, IF for discount logic, and ROUND for totals
VLOOKUP exercise: given a product catalog table, use VLOOKUP to populate prices from a product ID
Short answer: what does the FALSE parameter in VLOOKUP specify?

title: CSV Files and Data Interchange
obj: Students will be able to explain the CSV format, open and edit CSV files in a spreadsheet application, and describe common IT use cases for CSV data interchange.
content: comma-separated values; plain text; no formatting; universal compatibility
Text Import Wizard; delimiter selection; handling quoted fields with commas
modifying data; saving back as .csv vs. converting to .xlsx; encoding (UTF-8 vs. ANSI)
exporting user lists from AD, import/export of ticketing data, network device configs, log analysis
no formulas, no formatting, no multiple sheets; single data table only
TSV (tab-separated), pipe-separated; when to use each
assess: CSV import lab: open a provided CSV file in Excel/Sheets, clean up formatting issues, and save as .xlsx
IT use case: explain the steps to export an Active Directory user list to CSV and open it in Excel
Short answer: what problem can arise if a CSV field contains a comma, and how is it handled?

title: Advanced Spreadsheet Features and Data Analysis
obj: Students will be able to use advanced spreadsheet features including charts, pivot tables, conditional formatting, and data validation to analyze and present data.
content: column, bar, line, pie charts; choosing the right chart type; chart titles, labels, and legends
highlight cells based on rules (greater than, duplicates, top 10%); data bars; color scales
creating from a dataset; rows/columns/values/filters; summarizing data without formulas; refreshing
dropdown lists, number range restriction, date limits; input messages; error alerts
defining and using named ranges in formulas; readability benefits
Goal Seek; Data Tables for scenario comparison; Scenario Manager
assess: Chart exercise: given a monthly sales dataset, create a line chart with proper labels and a trend line
Pivot table lab: build a pivot table that summarizes sales by region and product category
Short answer: what is the purpose of conditional formatting in a data-heavy spreadsheet?

title: Presentation Software Fundamentals
obj: Students will be able to create a basic slide presentation with text, images, and transitions using presentation software.
content: slide panel, editing area, notes pane, view modes (Normal/Slide Sorter/Presenter)
title slide, title+content, blank; applying and changing layouts; master slide concept
text boxes, bullet points, images, icons, shapes; placeholder vs. free text box
applying themes; customizing colors and fonts; keeping design consistent
slide transitions (Fade, Push); object animations (Appear, Fly In); avoiding overuse
saving as .pptx vs. .pdf vs. Google Slides; exporting as video; presenter view controls
assess: Starter presentation: create a 5-slide presentation on any IT topic with title, 3 content slides, and a closing slide
Short answer: why is it recommended to use a pre-built theme rather than manually formatting every slide?
Design critique: given a poorly designed slide, identify three specific improvements

title: Creating and Producing Presentations
obj: Students will be able to design, structure, and deliver an effective slide presentation that communicates a clear message to a target audience.
content: opening hook, agenda, body (3-5 key points), conclusion, call to action
6×6 rule (max 6 bullets, 6 words each); one idea per slide; high-contrast text; readable fonts
using images over text; charts to replace tables; white space; visual hierarchy
writing notes for presenter use; difference between what the slide shows and what you say
alt text on images, sufficient color contrast, readable font size (min 24pt body), captions
rehearsing with slides, handling questions, exporting handouts, sharing via link vs. file
assess: Full presentation project: create and deliver a 7-10 slide presentation on an IT career topic to the class
Peer critique: evaluate a classmate's presentation using a design rubric (clarity, visuals, structure, delivery)
Short answer: what does the '6×6 rule' mean and why does it improve presentations?

title: Web Design Terminology and Interactive Media
obj: Students will be able to define core web design vocabulary, identify the roles on a web team, and describe how websites are structured and delivered.
content: client (browser) requests a URL; DNS resolves to IP; server returns HTML/CSS/JS; browser renders
design = visual/UX; front-end dev = HTML/CSS/JS; back-end dev = server/database; full-stack
wireframe, mockup, prototype, landing page, CTA, responsive design, viewport, above the fold
static vs. dynamic websites; forms, carousels, video embeds, maps, chatbots
PNG vs. JPG vs. SVG vs. WebP; web fonts (WOFF2); icon libraries
web hosting, domain registrar, DNS A records; FTP/SFTP upload; CMS vs. coded site
assess: Vocabulary quiz: match 15 web design terms to definitions
Website anatomy: label a provided website screenshot with 10 web design terms
Short answer: what is the difference between a wireframe and a mockup?

title: Visual Design Elements
obj: Students will be able to identify and apply the core visual design elements — line, shape, color, texture, space, and typography — in the context of web and digital design.
content: types (straight, curved, diagonal); implied lines; how lines guide eye movement
geometric vs. organic; how shapes communicate mood; using shapes in UI components
hue, saturation, value; warm vs. cool; emotional associations; color in branding
real vs. implied texture; using texture to add depth without clutter; background textures
positive vs. negative space; white space as a design tool; breathing room in layouts
serif vs. sans-serif; display vs. body fonts; type hierarchy (H1 → body); line length (50-75 chars)
assess: Element identification: annotate a provided web design screenshot, labeling each visual element
Color mood board: create a color palette for two contrasting brands (e.g., children's app vs. law firm) and explain your choices
Short answer: why is white space considered a design element rather than wasted space?

title: Design Principles
obj: Students will be able to apply the core design principles — contrast, alignment, repetition, proximity, hierarchy, and balance — to evaluate and improve digital layouts.
content: making important elements stand out; color contrast for accessibility (WCAG AA ratio); size contrast
grid systems; aligning elements to create order; left/center/right alignment choices
repeating colors, fonts, and shapes to create visual cohesion; style guides and design systems
grouping related items close together; separating unrelated items; gestalt principle
leading the eye from most to least important; using size, color, and placement to rank content
symmetrical vs. asymmetrical balance; visual weight; avoiding one-sided layouts
assess: Principle identification: analyze a provided web page and identify one example of each principle
Redesign exercise: given a poorly designed flyer, apply all 6 principles to improve it
Short answer: how does visual hierarchy guide a user's eye on a web page?

title: HTML Fundamentals
obj: Students will be able to write valid HTML using common tags to structure a web page with headings, paragraphs, links, images, and lists.
content: DOCTYPE, html, head, body; metadata (title, charset, viewport meta tag)
h1–h6 headings, p paragraphs, strong, em, br, hr; semantic meaning vs. visual appearance
a href for hyperlinks (absolute vs. relative URLs); img src and alt attribute; why alt is required
ul (unordered), ol (ordered), li items; nested lists
table, tr, th, td; thead, tbody; when to use tables (tabular data only, not layout)
form, input (text/email/checkbox/radio/submit), label, textarea, select; action and method attributes
assess: Coding exercise: hand-code a 4-section HTML page (bio, skills list, links, and a contact form) without any CSS
Tag identification: given 20 lines of HTML, identify the tag and purpose of each element
Short answer: why is the alt attribute on an img tag important for both accessibility and SEO?

title: Build a Basic Web Page
obj: Students will be able to build a complete, styled, multi-section web page combining HTML structure with CSS visual design.
content: inline vs. internal vs. external stylesheet; selector, property, value syntax; linking CSS to HTML
element, class (.), ID (#); specificity basics; descendant selectors
content, padding, border, margin; how box-sizing affects layout; inspecting with DevTools
font-family, font-size, color, background-color; Google Fonts import; rem vs. px
display block vs. inline vs. inline-block; flexbox for simple layouts; centering techniques
meta viewport tag; % widths; max-width; media query concept (not required to write)
assess: Web page project: build a personal IT portfolio page with a header, about section, skills list, and contact section — styled with external CSS
DevTools inspection: open any website, use browser DevTools to modify the CSS of one element and take a screenshot
Short answer: what does the CSS box model include and how does padding differ from margin?
